title: Closed-loop biophysical and computational simulator
subtitle: Solidifying the physical and computational foundation of the neural micro-probe simulator.
excerpt: An in-depth systems architecture and mechanical defense of the neural micro-probe simulator, modeling Euler buckling physics, CVD-deposition materials, and 3D array insertion mechanics.

content.text: My Reflection

This project bridges the gap between data orchestration and physical mechanics. The simulator isn't just about writing code; it’s about modeling the physical intersection of material science, tissue interaction, and real-time signal processing pipelines. Below is the blueprint of what I've built, the physics governing it.
1. The Core Physics: Euler Buckling

The heart of the simulator is governed by a single equation modeling physical failure: F_cr = (π² * E * I) / (K * L)². Let's break down the variables and their real-world application in bioelectromechanics:
• E (Young's Modulus): Material stiffness. There is a 200,000x difference between tungsten (411 GPa) and PDMS (2 MPa). This explains why tungsten microwires never buckle, while PDMS arrays do.

• I (Moment of Inertia): For a rectangular cross-section, this is (w * h³) / 12. Because thickness is cubed, doubling the probe thickness yields an 8x increase in buckling resistance. Takeaway: Probe designers prioritize thickness over width.
• L (Length): Unsupported shank length in tissue. Because length is squared in the denominator, doubling the length drops the buckling force by 4x. Longer probes carry exponentially higher risk.
• K (Effective Length Factor): Boundary conditions. Fixed-pinned = 0.7 (one end at the robot, the other in brain tissue).

Key Architectural Insight: The test_longer_probe_lower_buckling_force unit test mathematically proves the L² relationship. If questioned on why I selected 3mm over 5.5mm for short probes, this equation is the definitive answer.
2. The Material Library
The system currently models five distinct materials, each representing a specific era or methodology in neural interfacing:
• Parylene-C (3.2 GPa): The industry standard (used by Precision BioMEMS). Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)-deposited, conformal, pinhole-free insulation.
• PDMS (2 MPa): Ultra-soft silicone. Used as the designated failure case to demonstrate system limits and biological realism.
• PVAc-CNC (2 GPa): Nanocellulose-reinforced. The bleeding edge of bioelectromechanically compatible materials.
• Tungsten (411 GPa): The old school. High stiffness but causes chronic glial scarring (the primary reason Neuralink pivoted away from it).
• Silicon (160 GPa): Standard MEMS substrate. The foundational base before polymer coating.
3. Tissue Data: 2D vs. 3D Arrays

Empirical data sourced from real agarose phantom testing (0.6 wt% brain phantom, 2023 literature) reveals a stark contrast between planar (2D) and volumetric (3D) array insertions. 3D arrays require ~36% less insertion force and experience ~24% less shear. This physical mechanic is the underlying justification for heavy industry investment in 3D thread architecture.

chartData.title: 2D vs. 3D Array Mechanics (Agarose Phantom)
chartData.description: Comparative mechanical analysis showing insertion force and shear stress reductions at a depth of 1.5mm in 0.6 wt% agarose phantom.

content.text: 4. Data Streaming Architecture
The MockDataStream class mirrors production pipelines used by OpenEphys, SpikeGLX, and Neuralink by wrapping BrainFlow's SYNTHETIC_BOARD:
• Specs: 64 channels at 256 Hz (standard EEG).
• Pipeline: Callback-based architecture pushes samples into a rolling ring buffer (via deque), which is then converted to a pandas DataFrame via get_buffer().

• Synthetic Fallback: When BrainFlow is offline, the system generates artificial EEG signals mimicking spatial variation across cortical regions. Each channel receives a unique mix of frequency bands (e.g., Alpha 8Hz, Beta 20Hz, Theta 4Hz) layered with Gaussian noise.
5. Execution Roadmap
Phase 1 Immediate Priorities (Pre-June 2nd):
• [x] Euler Buckling: Internalize physical intuition to explain the concepts plainly.
• [x] Parylene-C: Skim core literature on CVD deposition and conformal coating in neural interfaces.
• [x] 3D Array Data: Memorize the 36% insertion force reduction stat as the primary defense for 3D architecture.
Phase 2 System Expansion (Next 2-3 Weeks):
• [ ] Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS): Research Randles circuits and double-layer capacitance for Subsystem 2.
• [ ] Signal Processing: Deep dive into EEG frequency bands, spike morphology (action potentials), and the Nyquist sampling theorem (30 kHz vs. 256 Hz).
• [ ] Theoretical Alignment: Connect the simulator's generated signals to the predictive processing/free energy principle framework.

title: Biomimetic Error Mitigation in Decentralized Networks
subtitle: Translating Quantum Error Correction Logic to Classical Systems Architecture
excerpt: This journal posts proposes a computational framework for translating the architectural logic of Quantum Error Correction (QEC) into classical, highly decentralized sensor networks.

content.text: Abstract

This journal post proposes a computational framework for translating the architectural logic of Quantum Error Correction (QEC)—specifically syndrome measurement and redundancy protocols—into classical, highly decentralized sensor networks. Environmental data corruption is modeled as an Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN) channel, and network-state uncertainty is tracked using a Shannon/von Neumann-style entropy analog. We implement the framework as a 2D parity product code decoded by Min-Sum Belief Propagation and benchmark it against standard classical baselines (No Recovery, Naive Majority Vote, Hamming [7,4], and a degree-matched random LDPC) on standardized Eb/N0 axes. At small block length (n=24), the biomimetic construction achieves classical-optimal decoding performance and outperforms a random LDPC ensemble of matched degree distribution (dv=2, dc=6), attributable to the regular topology suppressing short trapping cycles. At larger block lengths (n ≈ 120, 440) the structural advantage diminishes, consistent with the known asymptotics of product codes versus capacity-approaching random ensembles. The framework is thus positioned as a small-block-length architecture suitable for localized distributed systems, swarm sub-arrays, and last-mile sensor networks.
Keywords
Systems architecture, biomimetic networks, fault tolerance, product codes, LDPC, belief propagation, distributed computing.
1. Core Principles

1. Decentralized Syndrome Measurement: Classical network nodes perform redundant parity checks that mirror the role of quantum ancilla qubits, detecting data drift without interrupting primary network throughput or requiring a centralized global read.
2. Gaussian Noise Modeling: Environmental stressors and hardware degradation are modeled as an AWGN channel with variance parameterized by the signal-to-noise ratio Eb/N0.

3. Entropic State Tracking: Network uncertainty is tracked via an entropy analog that quantifies the mixedness of the joint state distribution, acting as an algorithmic trigger for localized recovery.

4. Autonomous Feedback Loops: A closed-loop decoding algorithm iteratively corrects corrupted nodes by propagating belief updates across a Tanner graph, without requiring a central controller in the steady-state recovery path.
2. Mathematical Foundations

Network State Uncertainty (Entropy Analog).
We define S(ρ) = -Tr(ρ log2 ρ), used conceptually to quantify the informational disorder of the network's joint state. For the classical network considered here, ρ denotes the joint probability distribution over node states represented as a diagonal density operator in the computational basis; this is a classical object, and the density-matrix notation is borrowed for continuity with the QEC literature rather than to imply quantum coherence between nodes.

AWGN Channel Model.
Transmitted symbols x_i ∈ {+1,-1} are corrupted as y_i = x_i + n_i, where n_i is normally distributed with variance σ² = 1 / (2R * Eb/N0), where R denotes code rate. This standard parameterization penalizes low-rate codes for their per-bit energy expenditure and enables fair cross-rate comparison.

Syndrome Parity Check (Classical Translation).
Each check node computes Z_parity = ⊕(x_i) over adjacent variable nodes in the Tanner graph. Non-zero syndromes localize corruption boundaries without decoding the full data word.

Optimization Objective (Future Work).
A hybrid variant of the framework admits a central management heuristic that minimizes L(θ) = Σ(y_i - ŷ_i(θ))² + λ||θ||² over a parameterized recovery policy θ. The results reported here are obtained without this term, operating in the fully decentralized regime.
3. Implementation Phases
Phase I: Simulated Noise Injection. Deployment of the AWGN simulation environment and systematic sweep over Eb/N0 levels to characterize the unprotected baseline.

Phase II: Decentralized Syndrome Flagging. Activation of the biomimetic redundancy protocol. Nodes autonomously exchange and cross-verify parity states, performing iterative Min-Sum Belief Propagation on the 2D product-code Tanner graph.

Phase III: Algorithmic State Recovery. Scale-up to larger topologies, integration with application-layer management heuristics, and comparison against structured LDPC ensembles at longer block lengths.
4. Experimental Methodology

All architectures are benchmarked on an AWGN channel with standardized Eb/N0 axes, over N = 2000 independent trials per operating point. Both Word Error Rate (WER) and Bit Error Rate (BER) are tracked. Architectures evaluated include No Recovery (n=16, R=1.00), Naive Majority Vote (n=48, R=0.33), Hamming [7,4] (n=28, R=0.57), Random LDPC (n=24, R=0.67), and the Biomimetic QEC code (n=24, R=0.67).

chartData.title: Architectural Cost-Benefit Profile
chartData.description: At rate R=2/3, the biomimetic architecture matches or exceeds majority vote structures while requiring half the physical nodes.

content.text: 5. Small-Array Performance (n=24)

1. Recovery of a classical code family from QEC-inspired reasoning. Applying localized syndrome parity logic autonomously yields a 2D product code, which is a member of the LDPC family. The biomimetic framing cleanly guides a designer toward codes that classical coding theory independently identifies as near-optimal.

2. Structural superiority over random ensembles at small block length. At Eb/N0 = 3.0 dB, the structured biomimetic Tanner graph attains WER = 0.074, outperforming a random LDPC of identical rate and degree distribution (WER = 0.137). We attribute this to the regular 2D topology avoiding short trapping cycles.

3. Efficient redundancy tradeoff versus majority-vote schemes. At rate R=2/3, the biomimetic architecture matches or exceeds the Word Error Rate of Naive Majority Vote at R=1/3 using half the number of physical nodes (24 versus 48), at the cost of an iterative rather than constant-time decoder.

chartData.title: Decoding Performance at Eb/N0 = 3.0 dB (Lower WER is Better)

content.text: 6. Scale Dependence Analysis

To characterize how the structural advantage evolves with block length, additional experiments were conducted at n ≈ 120 and n ≈ 440, comparing the 2D product code to edge-shuffled random LDPC codes of identical degree distribution.

Scale-Dependence Caveat. The biomimetic architecture exhibits a clear performance advantage in the small-block-length regime (n = 24). At larger block lengths (n ≈ 120, 440), the structural advantage diminishes. This is consistent with the well-known asymptotic behavior of product codes: the inherent minimum-distance growth of 2D grids is sub-optimal relative to capacity-approaching irregular LDPC ensembles as n → ∞.
The framework is therefore positioned as a small-block-length architecture. Its natural deployment target is localized distributed systems in which node count per sub-array is on the order of tens.
7. Discussion and Applications

The primary contribution of this work is a translation path, not a new code. QEC-inspired architectural reasoning independently recovers a classical code family that has been extensively studied in the coding theory literature. The value of the framing lies in its composability: designers of distributed systems operating under noise can reason about fault tolerance using the intuitive primitives of QEC while remaining firmly within the classical regime.

Concrete deployment targets include:
- Localized distributed sensor arrays (environmental monitoring clusters, IoT sub-meshes) where per-cluster node counts are on the order of 10–50.
- Swarm robotics sub-groups, where individual tactical groups are bounded in size for latency and coordination reasons.
- Last-mile distribution networks, in which a regional logistics graph consists of a small number of modular nodes subject to stochastic disruption.
8. Conclusion

By decoupling the architectural logic of quantum error correction from the physical constraints of cryogenic quantum hardware, this framework provides a lens through which designers of classical distributed systems can recover near-optimal small-block-length coding structures using QEC-style reasoning. The simulation results confirm that the translation path is sound in the regime of interest and honestly delimit the block lengths at which the structural advantage holds.

title: The Architecture of the AI-Dollar
subtitle: Hegemony, Substrate Fragility, and the Transition to a Post-Petrodollar Global Order
excerpt: The global financial architecture is currently undergoing a structural realignment unprecedented since the collapse of the Bretton Woods system in 1971.

content.text: 1. Introduction: The Strategic Pivot from Petro-Hegemony to Compute-Hegemony

The global financial architecture is currently undergoing a structural realignment unprecedented since the collapse of the Bretton Woods system in 1971. For over half a century, the absolute dominance of the United States dollar has been inextricably linked to the petrodollar system—an implicit, geopolitical arrangement wherein global hydrocarbon energy markets, priced exclusively in dollars, necessitated perpetual international demand for U.S. currency and, by extension, U.S. Treasury securities. Today, that foundational substrate is eroding. Geopolitical realignment, the overt weaponization of the dollar following the 2022 sanctions on Russia, and the emergence of alternative bilateral settlement networks have accelerated a subtle but undeniable de-dollarization in traditional commodity markets.

chartData.title: The Global Transition
chartData.description: Visualizing the structural realignment from physical hydrocarbon commodities to informational compute substrates.

right.label: Geographic Moat
right.value: Data Centers, Fab Plants & Sovereign IP

content.text: In response to this tectonic shift, a coordinated, albeit unstated, grand strategy has emerged from the nexus of the U.S. state apparatus, the Federal Reserve, and domestic technology conglomerates. The objective is the establishment and fortification of the "AI-dollar." The fundamental premise of this strategy relies on the United States achieving and maintaining absolute global dominance in frontier artificial intelligence (AI) computing infrastructure. By monopolizing the physical and digital infrastructure of the next general-purpose technology, the U.S. aims to capture global technological rents, ensure U.S.-denominated assets remain the highest-return investments globally, and synthesize an entirely new baseline for global dollar demand. In this emerging paradigm, global actors will hold dollars not primarily to purchase geographically constrained hydrocarbon energy, but to purchase cognitive labor, data processing, and sovereign digital infrastructure.

As of May 2026, the operationalization of the AI-dollar strategy is visibly accelerating. Unprecedented sovereign wealth flows from the Gulf states into U.S. AI capital expenditures, the aggressive deployment and equity conversion mechanisms of the CHIPS and Science Act, and the Trump administration’s sweeping executive orders categorizing frontier compute as critical national security infrastructure all serve this transition. U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has explicitly championed this framework, promoting dollar-backed stablecoins—via the recently enacted GENIUS Act—as the "internet-native payment rail" explicitly designed to buttress the dollar's status as the global reserve currency.

However, the foundation of this new substrate is structurally brittle, perhaps more so than any financial baseline established in modern economic history. While crude oil is geographically fixed, excludable, and easily hoarded by nation-states, AI capabilities constitute pure information—a non-rival, highly reproducible, and profoundly leaky asset. The compute substrate faces severe, compounding vulnerabilities: the rapid proliferation of open-weight models and industrial-scale model distillation that critically undermine capital moats, an escalating energy paradox that threatens global climate and regional grid stability, recursive bubble dynamics heavily concentrated in a handful of corporate-sovereign entities, and a macroeconomic environment burdened by entirely unsustainable sovereign debt.

This exhaustive research report provides a definitive mapping of the AI-dollar architecture as it stands in mid-2026. It dissects the operational mechanisms of this macroeconomic transition, meticulously outlines the severe vulnerabilities inherent in replacing a physical commodity substrate with an informational one, and concludes with a probability-weighted scenario analysis of the impending global financial endgame.
2. Operationalizing the Compute Substrate: The Fusion of State and Capital

The transition to the AI-dollar is not occurring organically through decentralized market forces; it is being aggressively engineered through a fusion of public policy, targeted sovereign wealth absorption, and hyper-concentrated corporate capital. This alignment represents the corporate-sovereign form at its most explicit phase, rivaling, if not exceeding, the monopolistic concentrations of the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
2.1 The Stargate Initiative and the Infrastructure Nexus

The most visible and capital-intensive manifestation of the AI-dollar's physical buildout is the Stargate Project. Formally announced in January 2025 by U.S. President Donald Trump, Stargate LLC operates as an American multinational joint venture uniting OpenAI, Oracle, Japan's SoftBank Group, and MGX—a state-backed technology investment fund from the United Arab Emirates. The venture’s stated objective is to deploy an unprecedented $500 billion into AI infrastructure within the United States by 2029, targeting a total power capacity of 10 gigawatts (GW) to support the next generation of artificial intelligence training and inference.

By late 2025, the Stargate initiative had accelerated significantly ahead of its initial deployment schedule, securing nearly 8 GW of planned capacity and over $450 billion in committed investment. The scale of this physical infrastructure defies historical comparisons in the technology sector. The flagship site in Abilene, Texas, which saw its first two buildings become operational in September 2025, is designed to ultimately consume 1.2 GW of continuous power—equivalent to the energy demands of roughly one million residential homes. Under a comprehensive 15-year lease agreement, Oracle is actively deploying over 450,000 NVIDIA GB200 AI GPUs at the Abilene campus alone, supported by an onsite construction workforce exceeding 6,400 personnel.

However, the sheer scale and capital intensity of Stargate has necessitated strategic operational pivots. The traditional model of first-party infrastructure ownership has proven financially untenable over the long term, even for entities backed by the deepest sovereign wealth funds. Consequently, OpenAI has increasingly transitioned toward a more flexible, capital-light approach, prioritizing long-term bilateral leasing arrangements with third-party cloud providers over direct real estate and hardware ownership. This strategic shift effectively transforms Stargate from a monolithic physical entity into an "umbrella" conceptual framework for a broader, decentralized compute strategy spanning multiple states and providers.

Despite these operational adjustments, the geographical footprint of the compute substrate continues its relentless expansion. New gigawatt-scale data centers have been formalized across Shackelford and Milam Counties in Texas, Lordstown, Ohio, and a massive Oracle-Vantage partnership site in Wisconsin. Furthermore, U.S. compute hegemony is actively being exported via the "OpenAI for Countries" initiative, establishing allied digital outposts globally. These include Stargate UAE, an initial 1 GW cluster in Abu Dhabi; sustainable, fully hydro-powered facilities in Narvik, Norway; advanced installations in the United Kingdom; and a newly announced $25 billion investment project situated in the Patagonia region of Argentina. This international infrastructure buildout serves a highly calculated dual purpose: establishing a physical, global monopoly over frontier AI training networks while simultaneously absorbing foreign sovereign capital into U.S.-domiciled and U.S.-controlled technological assets.
2.2 State-Capital Fusion: The CHIPS Act and Federal Equity Acquisition

The U.S. government has decisively abandoned the pretense of a purely free-market approach to digital infrastructure, embracing explicit state-capitalism to secure the hardware layer of the AI-dollar. The evolution and aggressive execution of the CHIPS and Science Act serves as the primary mechanism for this profound federal intervention.

In August 2025, the Trump administration fundamentally altered the relationship between the federal government and domestic semiconductor manufacturing. Rather than conventionally distributing $8.9 billion in previously allocated CHIPS Act and Secure Enclave cash grants to Intel Corporation, the administration converted those funds into a direct 10% non-voting equity stake in the company. In executing this maneuver, the administration systematically stripped away prior Biden-era conditional requirements—such as mandatory project labor agreements, union crew requirements for plant construction, and strict prohibitions on corporate stock buybacks—prioritizing raw production capacity, corporate agility, and national security imperatives above domestic labor and regulatory constraints.

This aggressive, direct state intervention yielded immediate and massive financial returns for the federal balance sheet. Driven by a surge in central processing unit (CPU) demand for agentic AI architectures, rumors of advanced manufacturing contracts with Apple and Google, and the explicit strategic backing of the executive branch, Intel's stock soared roughly 400% from its 52-week low. This historic market rally resulted in a paper profit of over $30 billion for the U.S. government in a mere 90-day window by April 2026. This maneuver explicitly ties the fiscal health and revenue streams of the United States government directly to the equity valuations of its foundational technology monopolies, permanently blurring the line between private corporate enterprise and sovereign power.
2.3 Executive Architecture: Mandating the Compute Substrate

The executive branch has further fortified the AI-dollar architecture through a series of sweeping directives designed to eliminate regulatory friction and mandate the adoption of U.S. technology. The Trump administration's AI Action Plan, operationalized through multiple executive orders in 2025, categorized frontier compute explicitly as critical national security infrastructure.

Executive Order 14141, "Advancing United States Leadership in Artificial Intelligence Infrastructure," mandates the acceleration of federal permitting for gigawatt-scale data centers and energy infrastructure, stating that the U.S. must prevent adversaries from gaining access to powerful future systems. Concurrently, the administration moved to aggressively override localized regulatory resistance. The "Eliminating State Law Obstruction of National Artificial Intelligence Policy" executive order preempts state-level AI regulations—specifically targeting state laws that mandate algorithmic auditing for ideological bias or discrimination—arguing that a patchwork of 50 different regulatory regimes paralyzes the industry and threatens U.S. supremacy in the global AI race.

Furthermore, the administration established an AI Information Sharing and Analysis Center (AI-ISAC) to merge private sector vulnerability data with federal intelligence, and launched the "Promoting the Export of the American AI Technology Stack" initiative. This export program acts as a modern Marshall Plan for compute, deploying full-stack U.S. AI solutions—hardware, models, and cybersecurity frameworks—to allied nations, thereby deeply embedding global partners within a U.S.-anchored technological order.
3. Digital Bretton Woods and Sovereign Wealth Absorption

If physical data centers and silicon fabrication facilities represent the hardware of the AI-dollar, digital currencies and sovereign wealth partnerships represent its software and liquidity mechanisms. The strategy actively seeks to capture the immense capital reserves of energy-producing states, redirecting them from traditional petrodollar recycling into the new compute substrate.
3.1 Stablecoins as the Internet-Native Payment Rail

The monetary transition requires a frictionless, borderless settlement layer capable of operating at the speed of machine-to-machine transactions. U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has articulated a highly calculated vision where digital assets operate as the foundational pillars of a new global financial architecture. Following the passage of the GENIUS Act in July 2025, Bessent highlighted that well-regulated, dollar-based stablecoins create an "internet-native payment rail that is fast, frictionless, and free of middlemen".

The strategic objective is profound: as autonomous AI agents increasingly conduct millions of micro-transactions per second for data scraping, API access, and compute power, these transactions will be settled exclusively in dollar-pegged stablecoins. Because these stablecoins must be fully backed by high-quality liquid reserves, their global proliferation triggers a mandatory, structural surge in demand for U.S. Treasury securities. By ensuring that the global AI ecosystem runs on American digital infrastructure and settles in digital dollars, U.S. monetary primacy is not merely preserved; it is hard-wired into the foundational layer of the 21st-century economy.
3.2 The "Abraham AI" Framework and Gulf State Capital

To fund the multi-trillion-dollar infrastructure requirements of the AI-dollar, the United States has successfully pivoted the strategic investments of Gulf petro-states. In 2025 alone, Gulf sovereign wealth funds deployed over $66 billion into AI and digitalization. This diplomatic and financial realignment has been dubbed the "Abraham AI" framework—utilizing the diplomatic normalization template of the Abraham Accords to achieve deep technological and economic integration.

Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund (PIF), operating through its subsidiary HUMAIN, launched a $10 billion VC fund dedicated to AI and partnered with NVIDIA to build massive "AI factories" in the Kingdom. These facilities, projected to reach 500 megawatts of capacity, will be powered by hundreds of thousands of NVIDIA's advanced Grace Blackwell superchips, establishing a sovereign AI infrastructure completely reliant on the American hardware ecosystem. HUMAIN additionally partnered with Elon Musk's xAI to construct data centers in the region, further cementing the alliance between Gulf capital and the U.S. techno-aristocracy.

Similarly, the United Arab Emirates has integrated deeply into the U.S. AI-dollar strategy. MGX, the UAE's technology investment vehicle chaired by National Security Advisor Sheikh Tahnoon bin Zayed Al Nahyan, became a founding equity partner in the $500 billion Stargate Project. Demonstrating the unprecedented fusion of sovereign wealth and U.S. political power, an investment vehicle backed by Sheikh Tahnoon also acquired a 49% stake in World Liberty Financial (WLF)—a crypto company owned by the Trump family—for $187 million just days before the 2025 presidential inauguration. This transaction, which triggered significant scrutiny from the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States (CFIUS), explicitly illustrates how foreign sovereign capital is actively financing both the technological infrastructure of the United States and the personal financial networks of its executive leadership.
4. The Structural Vulnerabilities of the Compute Substrate

While the strategic logic of the AI-dollar is coherent, the substrate upon which it is built is fundamentally brittle. Unlike crude oil, which is a physical, excludable, and exhaustible resource, AI is fundamentally rooted in information. The vulnerabilities of substituting a physical commodity with an informational one are severe and represent the critical failure points of the emerging macroeconomic paradigm.
4.1 The Information Brittleness Paradox: The DeepSeek Shock

The core assumption of the AI-dollar strategy is that the United States can maintain a durable monopoly on frontier intelligence by vastly out-spending global competitors on compute capacity. This assumes that capital deployment directly correlates with an unbridgeable capability gap. The events of early 2025 demonstrated the fatal flaw in this logic, providing a brutal lesson in the non-rivalrous nature of information.

In late December 2024 and January 2025, DeepSeek—an AI research laboratory funded by High-Flyer, a Chinese hedge fund—released its V3 and R1 models. DeepSeek-R1 delivered advanced reasoning capabilities that matched or exceeded the performance of heavily capitalized proprietary models, such as OpenAI's o1. The profound shock to the global system was not merely the capability, but the unprecedented cost efficiency: DeepSeek successfully trained the V3 baseline model for approximately $5.6 to $6 million in computing costs. Utilizing a highly optimized Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture with 671 billion parameters (but only 37 billion active during inference) and multi-head latent attention (MLA) mechanisms, DeepSeek achieved frontier performance at roughly 1/50th the cost of Western equivalents.

Furthermore, the release triggered an industry-wide reckoning regarding the practice of "model distillation." Investigations by major U.S. developers in February 2026 revealed that DeepSeek, alongside other Chinese labs like Moonshot AI and MiniMax, had engaged in industrial-scale extraction attacks against Western frontier models. Using tens of thousands of fraudulent accounts, these entities generated millions of interactions with models like Anthropic's Claude and Google's Gemini, systematically extracting reasoning capabilities, chain-of-thought processes, and agentic behaviors. DeepSeek integrated roughly 800,000 chain-of-thought examples distilled from proprietary models into the reinforcement learning pipeline for R1, drastically reducing the computational resources required for pre-training.

This dynamic exposes the inherent fragility of the AI-dollar substrate. A petro-state can physically hoard oil, secure it with military force, and tightly control its global diffusion. An AI-state cannot easily hoard capabilities. Information is non-rival; once intelligence is generated, it inevitably leaks through open weights, model distillation, and corporate espionage. The U.S. State Department recognized this threat, issuing a global directive in April 2026 warning allies about Chinese model distillation practices and the unauthorized replication of American AI systems. However, regulatory warnings cannot alter the mathematics of information. DeepSeek’s ability to offer inference API costs at 20 to 50 times cheaper than Western incumbents shatters the narrative that massive capital expenditure guarantees a permanent, monetizable monopoly. The very substrate the techno-aristocracy is betting on is structurally less hoardable than anything they have previously controlled.
4.2 The Energy Contradiction and Grid Saturation

The second profound vulnerability lies in the severe physical constraints of the digital realm. The AI-dollar substrate demands an exponential increase in electrical power generation, forcing an immediate, structural contradiction: the futuristic digital economy is entirely reliant on the rapid expansion of legacy energy systems, primarily fossil fuels.

Data center electricity consumption is projected to double by 2030, driven largely by the power-intensive demands of AI-optimized servers, which will account for 44% of total data center power consumption by the end of the decade. This crisis is most acute in Texas, the epicenter of the Stargate buildout. The Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT) released a preliminary Long-Term Load Forecast in April 2026 projecting that demand on the Texas grid will quadruple to an astonishing 367,790 megawatts (MW) by 2032—up from a historical peak of 85,508 MW in 2023. Of that projected 2032 capacity, massive data centers account for roughly 235,000 MW of the requested load.

The strain on transmission providers is immense. Oncor, a primary utility in Texas, projects its large-load submissions to jump from 5,018 MW in 2026 to nearly 110,000 MW by 2032. Consequently, Oncor has announced a $47.5 billion base capital plan for 2026–2030 to construct the necessary transmission infrastructure, including new 765-kV import paths. This immense capital requirement is triggering severe regulatory and public pushback regarding the "Who Pays?" conflict. As grid congestion costs rise exponentially, state utility commissions face intense pressure to protect residential ratepayers from cross-subsidizing the infrastructure required by trillion-dollar technology giants. Regulatory mechanisms, such as Virginia's "GS-5" tariff that forces large loads to pay 85% of transmission capacity costs during ramp-up, threaten to dramatically increase the operational expenditures of the AI substrate.

Crucially, this energy demand locks in carbon-intensive infrastructure for decades. To meet the voracious baseload appetite of gigawatt-scale data centers, utilities are actively delaying the retirement of coal plants and aggressively expanding natural gas generation facilities. While hyperscalers attempt to supplement with nuclear energy and renewables, the immediate power requirements of AI training far outpace the deployment timelines of green grids. Consequently, the AI-dollar strategy requires the very fossil infrastructure it is supposedly replacing in monetary terms, placing the United States on a collision course with global climate imperatives and rendering the substrate highly vulnerable to localized grid failures.
4.3 Hyper-Concentration and Late-Bubble Recursive Capital

The capital required to meaningfully participate in the frontier AI race has effectively eliminated traditional market competition, consolidating absolute power within a narrow oligopoly. This has birthed classic late-stage bubble dynamics characterized by recursive capital flows and hyper-concentrated equity valuations.

By 2025, technology sector capital expenditure targeting AI eclipsed $400 billion, remarkably surpassing the total global capital expenditure for oil and natural gas production. Projections for 2026 indicate a further surge toward $725 billion to $750 billion in AI infrastructure spending. This staggering wealth is circulating within a tightly closed loop: hyperscalers (such as Microsoft, Amazon, and Google) invest tens of billions into AI research startups (like OpenAI and Anthropic). These startups, contractually bound, use those funds to rent cloud compute from the hyperscalers and purchase hardware almost exclusively from NVIDIA. Concurrently, NVIDIA invests heavily in specialized cloud providers like CoreWeave, which in turn use that capital to purchase more NVIDIA chips.

This recursive flow has concentrated an unprecedented degree of global market capitalization into a single hardware provider. In early 2026, NVIDIA became the first company in history to cross the $5 trillion market capitalization threshold, accounting for an overwhelming percentage of the S&P 500's broader gains. NVIDIA's data center revenue—which constitutes roughly 90% of its business—is heavily reliant on a handful of hyperscale customers whose committed CapEx acts as a highly concentrated revenue floor.

These dynamics signal profound systemic risk. The equities market is currently pricing in perfect execution and perpetual exponential growth. However, tangible signs of "capex fatigue" are emerging among institutional investors. If the massive infrastructure investments do not quickly translate into scalable, high-margin software monetization, or if hyperscalers successfully pivot to custom, in-house silicon to break NVIDIA's monopoly, the recursive loop severs. The resulting implosion of NVIDIA's valuation would send catastrophic deflationary shocks through the broader equities market, undermining the foundational wealth supporting the AI-dollar strategy.

chartData.title: Macro Metrics: Tech/AI vs. Traditional Energy (2025-2026)

content.text: 4.4 The Productivity Mirage and the K-Shaped Economy

The ultimate macroeconomic justification for the unprecedented capital allocation toward AI is the promise of a massive, paradigm-shifting surge in global economic productivity. If AI functions as a true general-purpose technology, it should allow the U.S. economy to grow aggressively without reigniting inflation, validating the AI-dollar hypothesis and generating the wealth necessary to service the national debt.

Macroeconomic data from late 2025 and early 2026 presents a decidedly mixed reality that challenges the evangelist narrative. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported that nonfarm business sector labor productivity grew by 2.1% annually in 2025, experiencing a strong Q3 surge of 4.9% before moderating to 1.8% or 2.8% (depending on the revision) in the fourth quarter. Total factor productivity (TFP) increased by a modest 0.8% in 2025. Financial institutions like Goldman Sachs project that AI will help elevate the baseline potential GDP growth rate to roughly 2.1% through the end of the decade, marginally offsetting the profound headwinds of a shrinking U.S. labor force.

However, these gains do not reflect the broad-based, 5% to 10% utopian productivity miracle required to radically alter the U.S. fiscal trajectory. Instead, they indicate a highly concentrated, "K-shaped" economic outcome. While output has grown, aggregate hours worked have remained stagnant or fallen, indicating that corporations are leveraging AI primarily to automate tasks and reduce headcount rather than expand overall operations. Crucially, as of Q3 2025, the U.S. labor share of income—the fraction of the economy's income paid to workers—fell to a record low.

The realized productivity gains are heavily siloed within capital-intensive firms aggressively deploying AI, while the average worker and broader sectors of the economy fail to capture the economic benefits. Academic analyses, such as those from the Wharton Budget Model and the Budget Lab at Yale, caution that the aggregate macroeconomic effects of generative AI may ultimately be quite modest due to specific task-level constraints and the limits of AI's share of total economic activity. If the grand productivity narrative fails to materialize at the spectacular scale currently priced into equities, the asset bubble will collapse, leaving the AI-dollar substrate structurally hollow and devoid of fundamental support.
5. Macroeconomic Deterioration and the Geopolitical Hedge

The aggressive construction of the AI-dollar is not occurring in an era of fiscal strength, but rather against a backdrop of severe, accelerating sovereign deterioration. In structural terms, the dollar is increasingly backed by U.S. Treasury debt, which is backed by the expectation that the U.S. government can continue to issue more debt, which is ultimately backed by faith in the dollar. This closed, self-referential loop is fundamentally threatened by domestic fiscal realities and global geopolitical hedging.
5.1 The Debt Death Spiral

The United States is currently navigating a mathematically unsustainable fiscal trajectory. In late 2024, the gross national debt crossed $36 trillion, equating to roughly 122% of GDP. Even more alarming is the staggering cost required to service this debt in a higher-interest-rate environment. In fiscal year 2024, net interest payments on the national debt surpassed U.S. defense spending for the first time in nearly a century.

By 2025, net interest outlays nearly tripled from their 2020 levels, reaching $970 billion—becoming the second-largest line item in the federal budget after Social Security and definitively surpassing total spending on Medicare and national defense. Projections by the Congressional Budget Office (CBO) indicate that interest costs will eclipse $1.0 trillion in 2026 and surge to $2.1 trillion annually by 2036, consuming over 20% of all federal revenue. Over the next decade, the U.S. government is projected to spend a staggering $16.2 trillion solely on interest payments.

This crushing fiscal burden severely limits the government's macroeconomic flexibility and capacity to respond to future crises. As the U.S. Treasury is forced to issue unprecedented volumes of debt simply to service existing interest obligations, it risks crowding out private investment, elevating borrowing costs across the economy, and stoking long-term inflation. The AI-dollar strategy—which relies heavily on stablecoin reserves and foreign capital to perpetually purchase this debt—is essentially a high-stakes mechanism designed to artificially extend the runway of this deteriorating fiscal position.
5.2 De-Dollarization and the Golden Hedge

Global central banks are acutely aware of the fragility of the U.S. fiscal position and the geopolitical risks of relying solely on the dollar. The 2022 weaponization of dollar reserves and FX assets against Russia definitively demonstrated to non-aligned nations that dollar holdings carry severe sovereign risk. Consequently, a massive, structural wave of de-dollarization has manifested globally, primarily in the form of historical, relentless gold accumulation.

Central bank gold purchases shattered historical baselines, with net additions exceeding 1,000 tonnes annually in 2022, 2023, and 2024. While the pace moderated slightly in 2025 to 863 tonnes due to record-high gold valuations, the strategic shift remains robust and fully intact. Emerging market powers—particularly China, India, Poland, and Turkey—have driven this demand. The People's Bank of China actively sold off U.S. Treasuries while aggressively expanding its physical gold reserves, an explicit, coordinated maneuver to escape U.S. financial jurisdiction and mitigate future sanctions risk.

The culmination of this trend occurred in early 2026, marking a historic macroeconomic inflection point: for the first time since 1996, foreign central banks now hold a larger share of their international reserves in physical gold than in U.S. Treasuries. This crossover signifies that global reserve managers are explicitly prioritizing durability, portability, and geopolitical neutrality over the yield offered by U.S. sovereign debt. The "stealth erosion" of the dollar’s traditional reserve status places an extraordinary, perhaps insurmountable burden on the success of the AI-dollar to fill the widening geopolitical vacuum.
6. Systemic Risks and the Threat of the "Zero-Day" Shock

Beyond traditional financial metrics and energetic limits, the informational nature of the AI substrate introduces a unique, terrifying vector of systemic fragility: catastrophic, AI-driven cybersecurity failure. Because frontier AI models are rapidly integrating into the core operational architecture of global finance, logistics, and defense, an exploited vulnerability carries true existential risks for the substrate itself.

This theoretical risk transitioned into an acute reality in April 2026, centering on Anthropic's highly advanced, unreleased model, Claude Mythos Preview. Recognizing the severity of the threat, Treasury Secretary Bessent and Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell convened an emergency, closed-door meeting with the CEOs of the nation's largest systemically important banks—including Goldman Sachs, Bank of America, Citigroup, Morgan Stanley, and Wells Fargo. The summit addressed Anthropic's disclosure that the Mythos model possessed an unprecedented, autonomous capability to identify and exploit critical "zero-day" vulnerabilities across all major operating systems, software stacks, and web browsers.

The implications of models like Mythos are profound and destabilizing. In the traditional cybersecurity paradigm, organizations relied on an "exploit window"—a buffer period following the disclosure of a vulnerability allowing for patching and remediation. AI models like Mythos reduce that exploit window to near-zero, enabling offensive actors to weaponize decades of accumulated software complexity and exploit subtle flaws in minutes. Despite these dire warnings from federal regulators, major financial institutions like Bank of America continue to aggressively expand their deployment of such models across their enterprise networks, driven by the intense corporate pressure to realize productivity gains and reduce headcount.

If an autonomous AI model—or a foreign adversary leveraging distilled open-weight variants—successfully compromises the U.S. financial system's digital infrastructure, it would precipitate an immediate, blinding liquidity crisis. In such a scenario, the Federal Reserve's traditional crisis management tools, such as the extension of central bank swap lines to provide emergency dollar liquidity, would be severely tested. Swap lines functioned effectively in 2008 and 2020 because the underlying digital plumbing of the financial system was intact, and global trust in the dollar remained absolute. In a world where central banks are actively hedging their reserves into physical gold, and where the digital infrastructure itself is under autonomous siege, a catastrophic software failure could trigger an acute crisis where standard monetary interventions fundamentally fail.
7. Scenario Analysis: Unwinding the Substrate and Post-Democratic Governance

The architecture of the AI-dollar represents a high-stakes, historically unprecedented gamble by the corporate-sovereign alliance to preserve American hegemony in a post-petrodollar world. Based on the integration of massive capital flows, severe structural vulnerabilities, and deteriorating macroeconomic baselines mapped in this report, the transition over the next five to ten years will likely conform to one of the following scenarios, ranked by estimated probability:
1. Scenario Alpha: Slow Erosion and Multipolar Fragmentation (Highest Probability)

The most likely outcome is a protracted, grinding transition. The AI productivity surge materializes, but primarily as a K-shaped phenomenon that automates routine tasks, degrades the labor share of income, and enriches hyperscalers without fundamentally altering the 2% aggregate growth baseline required to outgrow the national debt. The $500B Stargate infrastructure is built, but fractured across complex leasing agreements and continually plagued by severe power grid bottlenecks in states like Texas and Virginia. Crucially, the "DeepSeek paradigm" holds true: information cannot be hoarded. Foreign adversaries and open-source communities continually distill U.S. proprietary models, commoditizing intelligence and destroying the monopolistic rent-seeking of Big Tech.

In this scenario, the AI-dollar partially succeeds through stablecoin integration, but it is insufficient to mask the crushing $2 trillion annual interest burden of the U.S. debt. Central banks continue to hoard gold and aggressively explore non-dollar trade settlements. The result is a slow, steady erosion of dollar dominance into a multipolar currency system, characterized by chronic volatility and reduced American hegemony, but stopping short of a catastrophic global collapse.
2. Scenario Beta: Acute Crisis with Successful State Intervention (Moderate Probability)

A sudden catalyst—such as the bursting of the NVIDIA capital expenditure bubble, or a massive cyber-contagion event akin to the Claude Mythos threat—triggers a sudden, 2008-scale liquidity crisis. Equity markets collapse rapidly as the return on investment for the $1 trillion AI infrastructure buildout fails to materialize.

The Federal Reserve acts aggressively, extending limitless swap lines and explicitly backstopping the technology monopolies deemed "too big to fail," representing the ultimate fusion of state and capital. The U.S. government takes further direct equity stakes in critical tech infrastructure, mimicking the 2025 Intel intervention on a massive scale. While the dollar weakens significantly and U.S. soft power is permanently scarred, the draconian emergency actions maintain the core stability of the global financial system, pivoting the U.S. fully into a managed, state-capitalist technocracy.
3. Scenario Gamma: Acute Crisis with Systemic Failure (Low-to-Moderate Probability)

In this scenario, an acute shock hits precisely as U.S. domestic political dysfunction reaches its zenith, or when the physical grid (e.g., ERCOT) suffers a catastrophic failure under the unbearable weight of gigawatt-scale data centers. As capital rapidly flees U.S. equities, the Treasury struggles to auction debt to cover its soaring interest payments.

When the Fed attempts to deploy emergency swap lines, non-aligned central banks—who have spent the last four years fortifying their physical gold reserves to outstrip their Treasury holdings—refuse to participate, opting instead to settle regional trade in local currencies or asset-backed alternatives. The informational AI-dollar substrate shatters, leading to a chaotic, unmanaged unwinding of U.S. financial hegemony, a sovereign debt crisis, and a severe, prolonged contraction in global living standards.
4. Scenario Delta: The Singular AI-Dollar Triumph (Lowest Probability)

The utopian vision of the techno-aristocracy is fully realized. The deployment of Stargate and subsequent infrastructure yields true artificial general intelligence (AGI) or near-AGI systems. Global productivity surges by 5% to 10% annually, functionally wiping out the U.S. debt burden through massive economic expansion and unprecedented tax revenues. The U.S. maintains absolute control over these frontier models, aggressively enforcing the export controls and IP protections outlined in the Trump administration's AI directives. Dollar-backed stablecoins achieve total global penetration, and the dollar transitions seamlessly from being backed by physical oil to being backed by the indispensable, monopolized intelligence that runs the global economy.
8. Conclusion

The deepest structural reality of the next decade is that if AI succeeds as the primary monetary substrate, the corporate-sovereign form reaches its most explicit and authoritarian phase. The entities that control AI capital are not pretending to operate within traditional democratic capitalism; they are explicitly engineering a post-democratic governance model reliant on absolute technological supremacy. The AI-dollar serves as the ultimate realization of this corporate-sovereign fantasy at a global scale.

However, the fatal flaw in this architecture is the inherent brittleness of information. Oil hoarded is oil controlled; information hoarded inevitably leaks. The rapid proliferation of open-weight models, the relentless efficiency of model distillation, and the democratization of reasoning capabilities prove that the very technology the techno-aristocracy is betting on is structurally less hoardable than anything they have controlled before.

The transition from the petro-dollar to the AI-dollar is actively underway, operationalized by the integration of sovereign wealth, hyperscale capital, and federal mandate. Yet, as the energy grid strains under impossible loads, the national debt compounds into a death spiral, and global central banks retreat to the ancient safety of gold, the viability of the AI-dollar rests entirely on the dubious assumption that capital concentration can indefinitely constrain the flow of information. The coming five-to-ten-year window will definitively prove whether the U.S. can successfully engineer a managed transition to a new informational baseline, or whether the sheer weight of its structural contradictions will fracture the global financial system entirely.

title: Latent Planning Meets Autoregressive Generation
subtitle: Training custom transformers to think before they speak
excerpt: What happens when you give a language model the ability to plan in latent space before committing tokens? I've been training custom transformer architectures on Apple MPS to explore exactly this — and the results challenge some assumptions about autoregressive generation.

content.text: Most language models generate text one token at a time, left to right, without any explicit planning mechanism. Each token is conditioned on everything before it, but the model has no way to 'look ahead' or structure its output before committing to the next word.

This is a fundamental limitation. When you write an essay, you don't start with the first word and hope the last paragraph makes sense — you plan. You have a thesis, an outline, a sense of where the argument needs to go. Current autoregressive models lack this entirely.
The Latent Planning Hypothesis

My research explores a simple but powerful idea: what if we give models a dedicated 'planning' step in latent space before they begin generating? Instead of immediately producing tokens, the model first produces a compressed plan — a latent representation of the overall structure — and then conditions its autoregressive generation on that plan.

I've been training custom transformer architectures on Apple Silicon (MPS backend) using PyTorch and HuggingFace. The architecture uses a contrastive loss function to ensure that the latent plan is meaningfully different from a simple summary, and attention map attribution to verify that the model actually uses the plan during generation.
Early Results

The early results are promising. Models with latent planning show improved coherence over long sequences, better adherence to complex instructions, and — most surprisingly — improved factual consistency. The plan seems to act as a soft constraint that keeps the model 'on track' even when the autoregressive process would otherwise drift.

There's still much work to do. The planning mechanism adds computational overhead, and it's not yet clear how to optimally balance plan quality against generation speed. But the direction feels right — giving models the ability to think before they speak is a step toward more reliable, more controllable AI systems.

title: On Invisible Problems
subtitle: Documenting the drift before the stink
excerpt: When I ask myself whether I can still be whatever I want to be, the honest answer isn't really about me. It's about whether there's some invisible problem quietly drifting in the system that makes that statement untrue for someone else.

content.text: Growing up, I was told what most kids hear: you can be whatever you want to be. It was a rich thing to say in 2009, in the middle of a financial crisis that had just proven how fragile our systems actually were. I didn't have the language for it then, but the phrase always felt incomplete — like it came with an asterisk no one wanted to print.
I think about that phrase the same way I think about the River Thames.

By the mid-1800s, London had optimized for growth the way any ambitious system does — fast, optimistic, willing to push its problems downstream. Literally. The river was where the city put what it didn't want to look at. Two people noticed. Edwin Chadwick spent years documenting the coming collapse in reports parliament ignored. John Martin painted apocalyptic scenes of cities overwhelmed by their own waste — not for money, but because he needed people to see it. Both were dismissed as alarmists. Then came the summer of 1858, when the stink from the Thames got so bad that parliament physically couldn't sit in session. The smell forced the redesign the arguments never could.

I love that story. Not because of the collapse — because of Chadwick and Martin. They knew. They said so. They were ignored. And they kept going long enough that when the system finally broke, the blueprint for the fix was already on the table.

That's the part I keep coming back to as an adult. When I ask myself whether I can still be whatever I want to be, the honest answer isn't really about me. It's about whether there's some invisible problem quietly drifting in the system that makes that statement untrue for someone else. For a lot of someones.
That question is how I ended up in tech for good.

I didn't pick systems thinking because it was going to make me money. I picked it because I want to be the kind of person Chadwick and Martin were — someone who documents the drift before the stink, and keeps building even when no one's listening yet. Not because they were better than the parliament that ignored them, but because they cared enough to outlast being dismissed.
If I could hand the next generation one phrase to replace the one I grew up with, it wouldn't be you can be whatever you want to be. It would be a question:
What's something you wish you could do for your friend that you don't think they would notice?
That's where the work is.

title: Family, Friends, and Gemeinschaft
subtitle: A reflection on nostalgia, third places, and societal shifts
excerpt: I wonder now, as an adult, where do I return to in order to celebrate my existence in a place that offers no permanence. Is it a loss of remembrance that I'm grieving?

content.text: When I was a child I remember my birthday party vividly. It was at a McDonald's playground in the early years of 2003. Bush was President, and the net worth to own a burger for a meal didn't cost the stock of a new startup. I wonder now, as an adult, where do I return to in order to celebrate my existence in a place that offers no permanence. Blockbuster offered free rentals for every good grade you earned. Toys R Us gave you discounts on your birthday. Now I'll be lucky if I even wake up having someone text me happy birthday without Facebook reminding them. Is it a loss of remembrance that I'm grieving?

Gemeinschaft / Gesellschaft (Tönnies). Not community vs. society in the colloquial sense. Gemeinschaft is a mode of association based on natural will, kinship, shared locale, and unconscious tradition. Gesellschaft is based on rational will, contract, exchange, and instrumental calculation. I had the will to explore a place that offered no protection. We often feel as if these third places, coined by Oldenburg (1989), truly made us feel as if there was social neutral ground. A toy store on a child's birthday was heaven on earth. It was a place with no hierarchy, a place that was sacred. I don't believe they went away because they became inconvenient. Eventually, as I got older, a DVD was now megabytes in flash memory, practically invisible, with the same richness in playback. Incentives misaligned.

The mid-century compromise (1945–1975) offered the working and middle class a version of access: home ownership, pensions, union protection, public universities, civic organizations, shared physical infrastructure like public pools and libraries. The elite never agreed to share infrastructure permanently. They offered a temporary settlement and then rolled it back.
So I ask myself as an adult: do I remember myself in the moment with my friends, or do I miss the idea of thick places saturated with memory, relationship, habit, and affect?
